ch_egor's blog

Pinely Round 2 Editorial
By ch_egor, 3 years ago,

Thanks for the participation!

1863A - Channel was authored by Endagorion and prepared by irkstepanov

1863B - Split Sort was authored by Endagorion and prepared by ch_egor

1863C - MEX Repetition was authored by Endagorion and prepared by amethyst0

1863D - Two-Colored Dominoes was authored by Endagorion and prepared by AndreySergunin

1863E - Speedrun was authored by Endagorion and prepared by Golovanov399

1863F - Divide, XOR, and Conquer was authored by Endagorion and prepared by ch_egor

1863G - Swaps was authored by Endagorion and prepared by zemen

1863H - Goldberg Machine 3 was authored by Endagorion and prepared by Endagorion, irkstepanov

1863I - Redundant Routes was authored by Endagorion and prepared by Endagorion, irkstepanov

1863A - Channel

Solution

1863A - Channel

Let pp be the total number of + signs in the notification string. If a+p<na+p<n, then the answer is clearly "NO".

Now let's look at the current number of users online. Initially there are aa of them. Each notification either increases this number by 11, or decreases it by 11. If at some moment of time the number of users online is nn, then all users are online, thus, everyone must have read the post. The answer is "YES" in this case.

Otherwise the answer is "MAYBE". Indeed, suppose that the number of users online never hits nn, but a+p≥na+p≥n. On the one hand, if the first (n−a)(n−a) notifications of the + type correspond to new users, then all subscribers will have read the post. On the other hand, a situation when the nn-th user never log in is also possible.

1863B - Split Sort

Solution

1863B - Split Sort

For every kk such that ai=kai=k, aj=k+1aj=k+1 and i>ji>j we have to choose x=k+1x=k+1 at least once for these elements to be in the correct order.

It is easy to see that if we choose all such x=k+1x=k+1 for all such kk exactly once in any order, we will get the identity permutation.

1863C - MEX Repetition

Solution

1863C - MEX Repetition

Append the initial array a1,…,ana1,…,an with an+1=MEX(a1,…,an)an+1=MEX⁡(a1,…,an). It is easy to see that a1,…,an+1a1,…,an+1 is a permutation of 0,1,…,n0,1,…,n. In this case setting ai=MEX(a1,…,an)ai=MEX⁡(a1,…,an) is basically equivalent ai=an+1ai=an+1, but after this the new MEX(a1,…,an)MEX⁡(a1,…,an) is changed. In fact, it becomes equal to the old value of aiai, since this value is not now present in the array.

In other words, performing the operation on aiai is equivalent to swapping aiai and an+1an+1. Performing the operation on i=1,2,…,ni=1,2,…,n can be viewed as nn swaps. It means that the array changes from [a1,a2,…,an+1][a1,a2,…,an+1] to [an+1,a1,a2,…,an][an+1,a1,a2,…,an], i. e. we simply perform the cyclic shift to the right.

To finish up the solution, first, calculate an+1=MEX(a1,…,an)an+1=MEX⁡(a1,…,an) (you can do so, for example, by setting an+1=n(n+1)2−∑i=1naian+1=n(n+1)2−∑i=1nai). Second, calculate k%(n+1)k%(n+1) and then perform the cyclic shift in O(n)O(n).

1863D - Two-Colored Dominoes

Solution

1863D - Two-Colored Dominoes

Let's consider the requirement on the rows. Clearly, all horizontal dominoes (since each of them has 11 black and 11 white cell) do not influence the black-white balance for the rows. Thus, we are only interested in vertical dominoes.

Consider the first row and the vertical dominoes that intersect this row. Their number has to be even, otherwise, the first row has an odd number of cells covered by dominoes and the solution is clearly impossible. But if there is an even number of such dominoes, we have to paint half of them black-white, and half of them white-black. What's more, it doesn't actually matter the exact order we paint them in, because vertical dominoes do not affect the columns' balance and we will not influence the balance of the second row anyway. So we can freely paint them however we like. The same logic applies for rows 2,…,n2,…,n.

Now we turn to horizontal dominoes. In the first row, there once again has to be an even number of dominoes which intersect this column. And we can paint half them black-white, and half of them white-black, and it doesn't matter which exact way we choose. Do the same for columns 2,…,m2,…,m.

1863E - Speedrun

Solution

1863E - Speedrun

First of all, assume that the first quest we complete is at the hour xx. We can assume that 0≤x<k0≤x<k, as increasing it by kk just shifts all the times by kk. In this case one can greedily find the completion times for all the quests: essentially, for every quest vv, if we know that the quests it depends on are completed at hours c1c1, ..., cdcd, then the quest vv cannot be completed earlier than max(c1,…,cd)max(c1,…,cd). So if we define f(x,y)f(x,y) to be the smallest z≥xz≥x such that z≡y(modk)z≡y(modk), then the completion time of vv is f(max(c1,…,cd),hv)f(max(c1,…,cd),hv). If the quest vv does not depend on anything, then, obviously, the time if simply f(x,hv)f(x,hv).

The problem is that we don't know which starting time xx is optimal. On the other hand, we know that it can be assumed to be from [0,k)[0,k). Also, there is no sense in xx not being hvhv for any quest vv without incoming dependencies. So we can do the following: first assume that x=0x=0 and find all the completion times for the quests, denote them by cici. Then consequently increase xx until it becomes kk.

Sometimes xx becomes equal to some hvhv where vv is a quest that could theoretically be completed first. At these moments we know that the answer is no more than maxci−xmaxci−x. When we increase xx again, such quests can no longer have ci=xci=x, and thus some values cici increase by some multiple of kk.

However, for x=kx=k all cici are exactly kk more than when x=0x=0. Therefore, each value of cici in this process increases exactly once and exactly by kk. Now there are two ways to finish the solution.

  1. Sort all events of type "some cici, where quest ii doesn't have incoming dependencies, increase by kk". For each such event, we run DFS from such vertices to see if some the quests depending on them must also be completed kk hours later. One can see that this DFS will be run from each vertex exactly once throughout the process, thus resulting in O(n+m)O(n+m) time complexity.
  2. When building all cici the first time, also find for each vertex vv the first xx when its cvcv must change. If we denote it with wvwv then it can be expressed as max{wu:u→v,cu+k>cv}max{wu:u→v,cu+k>cv}. Then sort all events of type "some cici, where quest ii may or may not have incoming dependencies, increase by kk".

In both approaches one can easily maintain maxcimaxci as this value can only increase.

1863F - Divide, XOR, and Conquer

Solution

1863F - Divide, XOR, and Conquer

Let s=al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ars=al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ar, x=al⊕al+1⊕…⊕akx=al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ak, y=ak+1⊕ak+2⊕…⊕ary=ak+1⊕ak+2⊕…⊕ar.

If ss is zero, than we can choose any kk and any side because s=x⊕y=0⟹x=ys=x⊕y=0⟹x=y.

If ss is not zero we can choose such kk for the left side that the most significant bit of ss is the same as the most significant bit of xx because y=s⊕xy=s⊕x does not have this bit, and therefore less than xx. Same for the right side.

We will iterate over all subarrays in non-increasing order of length and will calculate two set of bits mask_startimask_starti and mask_endimask_endi for each position of array — bits, one of which must contain the XOR of subarray numbers to start/end at that position.

To check that we can achieve ll, rr we need to check that s∧mask_startl>0s∧mask_startl>0 or s∧mask_endr>0s∧mask_endr>0 or any segment can start/end in these positions, since there was a segment with zero XOR with such start/end.

If we can achieve [l;r][l;r] than:

  • if s≠0s≠0 add the most significant bit of ss to mask_startlmask_startl and mask_endrmask_endr;
  • if s=0s=0 remember that any subarray of shorter length can start/end at these positions.

The time complexity is O(n2)O(n2), the space complexity is O(n)O(n).

1863G - Swaps

Solution

1863G - Swaps

Consider a directed graph with nn vertices, where for each vertex ii there is an edge i→aii→ai. This is a functional graph, that is, every vertex has exactly one edge outgoing from it. Let's see how our operations affect the graph.

We will write u→vu→v to illustrate the fact that au=vau=v. Call the operation swap(ai,aai)swap(ai,aai) picking vertex ii. We can assume that we never picked such uu that the operation didn't change the permutation, that is, u→v→vu→v→v.

We will mark some edges in the graph as bold. Initially no edge is bold. When we pick vertex vv, one of the following can happen:

  • If no bold edges pass into vv, go along the bold edges until we can. Let's say the vertex we reached is uu. Then mark the edge from uu bold.
  • If vv has an incoming bold edge, do nothing.

At every moment of time, the graph corresponds to the permutation in the following way: if vv has an incoming bold edge then av=vav=v, otherwise, if uu is the vertex we reach by going from vv along the bold edges, and u→wu→w, then av=wav=w. Also, one can see that according to the algorithm of making edges bold no vertex ever has more than one incoming bold edge.

It is clear that each valid subset of bold edges corresponds to a unique permutation. However, one can see that the same permutation can correspond to multiple subsets. In particular, for a component of the graph, if av=vav=v for all vertices of the cycle in this component, then the boldness of all edges outside the cycle is uniquely determined, but for the edges of the cycle any subset of these edges that doesn't include exactly one of them corresponds to this permutation. To avoid this uncertainty, we say that this permutation will correspond to the subset of bold edges where all the edges from the cycle are bold (this is an invalid subset, because there is no sequence of operations that would mark it bold).

Now we want to calculate the number of possible subsets of bold edges. Each vertex can have either no incoming bold edges (there is 11 way to do this) or any one of them can be bold (invinv ways to do this for vertex vv). So the answer could be just ∏v(inv+1)∏v(inv+1). However, since some of the configurations are invalid, we need to modify the answer.

In particular, consider any cycle c1→…→ck→c1c1→…→ck→c1. All excluded subsets of edges correspond to exactly one of ci+1ci+1 having either no bold edge incoming, or any edge except ci→ci+1ci→ci+1. Therefore, the number of ways to choose the bold edges among the ones incoming to this cycle is

∏i=1k(inci+1)−∑i=1kinci.∏i=1k(inci+1)−∑i=1kinci.

Thus, the final answer is

∏cycles(∏i=1k(inci+1)−∑i=1kinci)⋅∏other v(inv+1).∏cycles(∏i=1k(inci+1)−∑i=1kinci)⋅∏other v(inv+1).

1863H - Goldberg Machine 3

Solution

1863H - Goldberg Machine 3

Let's consider a dynamic programming approach: for leaves, set dpv=avdpv=av, and for an internal vertex vv with children uu and ww, set dpv=2max(dpu,dpw)−[dpu≠dpw]dpv=2max(dpu,dpw)−[dpu≠dpw] we use an indicator notation […][…].

Let dv=dpv−1dv=dpv−1, then the update becomes dv=2max(du,dw)+[du=dw]dv=2max(du,dw)+[du=dw]. Let hvhv denote the distance from the root to vv, and Dv=dv⋅2hvDv=dv⋅2hv. Then Dv=max(Du,Dw)+2hv[Du=Dw]Dv=max(Du,Dw)+2hv[Du=Dw].

Notice that the binary representation of DvDv contains at most log2A+log2nlog2⁡A+log2⁡n ones (A=maxaiA=maxai). This can be easily proven by induction: suppose the minimum size of a tree that can result in DvDv having kk ones is f(k)f(k). Then, f(k)=1f(k)=1 when k≤log2Ak≤log2⁡A, and f(k)≥2f(k−1)f(k)≥2f(k−1) otherwise, since DvDv must be constructed from equal DuDu and DwDw from independent subtrees.

We'll maintain a set SS of pairs (v,D)(v,D), where DD is a lower bound on DvDv. Initially, put pairs (v,(av−1)⋅2hv)(v,(av−1)⋅2hv) into SS for all leaves with av>0av>0, and if there are (v,D)(v,D) and (u,D)(u,D) in SS (v≠uv≠u), put (w,D+2hw)(w,D+2hw) into SS, where w=lca(v,u)w=lca(v,u). The answer is 1+1+ the maximum DD among all pairs in SS (if SS is empty, the answer is obviously 00).

Start with an empty SS and add all the leaves to it. A new pair (v,D)(v,D) can create at most one new lca with pairs (u,D)(u,D), and at most one new pair (w,D+2k)(w,D+2k), where kk is necessarily a new bit. The process of creating new pairs continues, but it will finish in O(logA+logn)O(log⁡A+log⁡n) iterations since there cannot be more bits. Different DD values can be stored in a trie based on bit positions from most significant to least significant (or by some hashing), and vertex sets in the trie nodes can be stored in the order of entry time.

To change avav, remove the old pair from SS (similarly to adding) and add the new one. All together requires O(logn⋅(logA+logn))O(log⁡n⋅(log⁡A+log⁡n)) operations.

1863I - Redundant Routes

Solution

1863I - Redundant Routes

Let's call two paths adjacent if one can be obtained from the other by adding an edge to one end and removing an edge from the other end.

Proposition. There exists an optimal solution in which each pair of adjacent paths is either simultaneously chosen or simultaneously not chosen.

Proof. Consider an optimal solution that maximizes the total length of paths in it. Let's assume that in such a solution, we have taken some path abab, but its adjacent path cdcd is not included (here, assume a∉cda∉cd and d∉abd∉ab). Why can't we add cdcd?

  • If cdcd contains a shorter path, it must end at dd (since all other paths are also contained in abab). In that case, we can extend the shorter path eded to cdcd, which contradicts the maximization of the sum of lengths.
  • If cdcd is contained within a longer path, it must start at cc (since all other paths are also contained in abab). In this case, we can extend abab.
We will refer to classes of adjacent paths as simply classes. Each class will either be taken in its entirety or not taken at all, and according to the proposition above, an optimal solution can always be found.

In the rooted tree, we'll call a class vertical if it contains at least one vertical path. To reduce the number of vertical classes, we'll root the tree by the midpoint of its diameter (if the diameter is of odd length, consider the root as an edge). Now, all vertical paths of the same length are equivalent (since a vertical path can always be moved to another subtree of the root). Thus, for each length, there is at most one vertical class.

How do we determine if a path belongs to some vertical class? We'll attempt to greedily pull the path: first lower one end of the path to the deepest available vertex, then the other end, and so on. In the end, either the path becomes vertical or we encounter an obstacle of the following form: the endpoints are in non-intersecting subtrees of the same depth hh, whose roots are at distance dd apart, and the path length is h+dh+d. Moreover, any such pair of subtrees describes a non-vertical class of paths, and no other classes exist, as paths from a non-vertical class must hit such an obstacle.

Note. A class doesn't necessarily consist of all paths between subtrees of length h+dh+d. This is because an obstacle might exist lower down for some of these paths. Nonetheless, at least one representative for any pair of subtrees will definitely exist (for instance, the root of one subtree and the deepest leaf of the other).

Let's define non-vertical classes with a pair of root vertices of subtrees (v,u)(v,u) (with the convention tin(v)<tin(u)tin(v)<tin(u)), and define the class length as L(v,u)=h+dL(v,u)=h+d. We'll introduce a forest structure on non-vertical classes: a pair (v,u)(v,u) is an ancestor of (v′,u′)(v′,u′) if vv is an ancestor of v′v′ and uu is an ancestor of u′u′. All classes, together with this forest, can be constructed using DFS in O(n2)O(n2) time, simultaneously traversing vv and uu. Moreover, we can quickly compute the size of each such class. Let S(v,u)S(v,u) denote the number of all paths of length L(v,u)L(v,u) between subtrees vv and uu. Then, the size of class N(v,u)N(v,u) is S(v,u)−∑(v′,u′)N(v′,u′)S(v,u)−∑(v′,u′)N(v′,u′), where the summation is over all classes (v′,u′)(v′,u′) that are proper descendants of (v,u)(v,u) such that L(v′,u′)=L(v,u)L(v′,u′)=L(v,u). Thus, we need to compute all S(v,u)S(v,u). If we compute this for each class in O(h)O(h) time, the total time will be O(n2)O(n2) (exercise for the reader).

Which non-vertical classes can't be taken simultaneously? If between classes a=(v,u)a=(v,u) and b=(v′,u′)b=(v′,u′) neither is an ancestor of the other, then nested paths can't exist in them (due to the construction of the forest, there's a branching somewhere that's common to all paths).

Proposition. Let class aa be an ancestor of class bb. Then, a pair of nested paths between them can exist if and only if for all classes cc on the path between aa and bb (including bb but excluding aa), L(c)>L(a)L(c)>L(a).

Proof. Paths within bb cannot come out of subtrees v′,u′v′,u′. If L(a)≤L(b)L(a)≤L(b), then a path of length L(a)L(a) containing something from bb must also go between subtrees v′,u′v′,u′, but it can't extend beyond subtrees v,uv,u (since shorter paths from bb can't). Thus, it can't belong to aa. Otherwise, if we attempt to pull a path of length L(a)L(a) nested within bb upwards until encountering an obstacle at aa, this can only happen if aa is the first significant obstacle.

How do we include vertical classes? Clearly, we can take at most one vertical class. Using similar reasoning as above, if aa is a non-vertical class and ℓℓ is the length of a vertical class, then aa can be taken with a vertical class if there are no classes cc among the ancestors of aa (including aa) with L(c)≤ℓL(c)≤ℓ. Constructing a bamboo structure out of all vertical classes (shorter classes on top) and attaching the roots from the forest of non-vertical classes at the bottom of this bamboo, the criterion remains the same as in the proposition. Now we can forget about the original problem and solve a new one: given a rooted tree, with numbers LvLv and NvNv assigned to each vertex, we need to select a set of vertices to maximize the sum of NvNv, while satisfying the criterion.

Consider the vertex vv with the minimum LvLv among all vertices (considering the deepest among such vertices, if there are several options). It's easy to see that:

  • Vertices outside the subtree rooted at vv cannot prevent us from choosing vertices within the subtree rooted at vv.
  • If we choose vv, we can't choose anything else in its subtree.

Divide the set of all vertices VV into Tv−vTv−v and V−TvV−Tv, where TvTv is the subtree rooted at vv, and solve the problem independently for them. The final answer will be ans(V−Tv)+max(Nv,ans(Tv))ans(V−Tv)+max(Nv,ans(Tv)).

This process can be conveniently done in reverse, for instance, using DSU. It's also possible to carefully determine, during the forest construction, which components will merge, and solve the problem in O(n2)O(n2) time using just bucket sort without any additional data structures or sorting.

Tutorial of Pinely Round 2 (Div. 1 + Div. 2)

    Benny20
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    It says that if the pair of values (k,k+1)(k,k+1) appears inverted in the input permutation, then you need to perform the operation with x=k+1x=k+1 in order to get rid of that inversion. Now, just note that if you consider all these pairs and perform the operation when needed, starting from k=2k=2 until k=nk=n, the permutation will be ordered.

    → Reply

  • »

    Has_Aim
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Has_Aim
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    let's start from k = 1 and see if the (k + 1) position comes after the k position we stop when the condition fails and count it as a turn we chose x as the last item that we get, then we pick another k just the last item we didn't get from the previous turn, Each time we write a set of numbers in the correct order as much as we can, So the answer is the number of turns. To do that just count how many pos[i] > pos[i + 1] for all i from 1 to n — 1. It's exactly this problem : Collecting Numbers

    → Reply

      it can further be optimised as number are 1 — n, we can just place the index of x to the index x in sorted array

  • »

    OldBrilliantMate
    3 years ago, show (+4) #
    »
    »
    OldBrilliantMate
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +19

    Let, firstly, solve simpler problem. Assume starting time is 00 (meaning the first imaginary quest which is prerequisite of any other quest was done at day 0, hour 0). Maintain a dpdp array, where dp[v]dp[v] is the day when the quest vv is completed. It's clear that if the quest has no prerequisite it's dpdp value is zero. Otherwise, let u1,...,uku1,...,uk be prerequisites of vv, then dp[v]=max(dp[ui]+f(ui))dp[v]=max(dp[ui]+f(ui)) where f(x)=1f(x)=1 if h[x]>h[v]h[x]>h[v] and f(x)=0f(x)=0 otherwise. Answer to the problem is maximum value of dp[v]∗k+h[v]dp[v]∗k+h[v] (Since we are counting starting time as 00 and vv's quest is done after dp[v]dp[v] days on h[v]h[v] hours). However, we need to choose optimal starting time and somehow maintain the answer. Notice that optimal starting time is some h[i]h[i], where ii's quest has no prerequisite. Assume v1,...,vkv1,...,vk are such quests and WLOG assume h[v1]<=...<=h[vk]h[v1]<=...<=h[vk]. We will update starting time from 00 to h[v1]h[v1], from h[v1]h[v1] to h[v2]h[v2] and so on to h[vn]h[vn]. Let assume we are making transition from h[vi]h[vi] to h[vi+1]h[vi+1]. So firstly we must update dp[vi+1]dp[vi+1], then we mist update it's children. So we can simple use DFS. Also notice that every quest will be updated only once, so it should pass the time limit.

    → Reply

Thanks for this nice round, and fast editorial!

Here is my advice about the problems:

A

Great problem A ! Simple statement, simple solution (that still requires some idea), simple implementation, not a "formula" problem, perfect!

B

Creating a good div2 B is very hard but I think that this one does a great job! It is also very educative as showing an upper bound/lower bound is quite standard so yeah, great problem

C

I enjoyed this problem a lot but maybe it is a bit too easy for its rank ? Simulating the process by hand with n=4 is enough to find the solution.

D

It is probably a bit too easy for its rank? The problem is quite cool though

E

Really really great problem! Bonus point for the story

F

Seems like a cool problem, I couldn't solve it but it is enjoyable to try to solve it

Also, could you please clarify a bit the second part of the editorial of F please ? Maybe I'm just bad at english but I don't understand what mask_start and mask_end correspond to

Thanks :)

    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +11

    For point ii, if the xor of an interval [i,r[[i,r[ contains a bit of maskStartimaskStarti, then it means it is obtainable, since at one point prior, an interval [i,r′[[i,r′[ with r′>rr′>r had that bit for most significant bit of the xor. Same for maskEndimaskEndi.

    So you keep these 2N2N masks as you iterate over subarrays in length non-increasing to know which ones are obtainable.

    By the way I love your profile picture, where is it from ?

Soultaker
3 years ago, show (+6) #
»
Soultaker
3 years ago, hide # |
 
+17

For problem E, I didn't understand the editorial, and most other solutions seemed to use some form of Dynamic Programming, which seems different from what I did.

I start by calculating the maximal end time when you start questing at the earliest possible time, which is easy to calculate in O(V + E) time using the topological ordering of the DAG. However, this might not be optimal: it can be better to defer some quests to day 2 so we can start later in day 1, without affecting the end time. So I consider all the starting times of quests that have no dependencies. The key is that I update the end times for other quests incrementally, for an overall O(V + E log V) solution.

Submission with a (too many) comments: 221200048

→ Reply

      Soultaker
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      I tried to explain that in the last two paragraphs of the comments at the top of the file. It's not immediately obvious, but for each vertex v, we increase end_time[v] at most once, by exactly K. That means it's actually just O(V + E log V) time overall, not O(V + VE log V) as it would otherwise be.

      And tfg pointed out, correctly, that the priority queue is not needed, so instead of O(E log V) it could be something like O(E), but the really important part is that it's not O(VE), which would be much too slow to pass. The difference between O(E) and O(E log V) is relatively small.

      → Reply

Why I didn’t solve F

First I saw the n≤104n≤104 constraint so I went for an O(n2)O(n2) solution. But then, I realized that Codeforces problems rarely have O(n2)O(n2) problems with nn bigger than 50005000. So I gave up immediately and went for an O(602×n)O(602×n) solution, which is a bit smaller and could definitely pass the problem.

    0

    Here's my wrong idea on F: (maybe it's right, I don't know)

    Let s(l,r)=al⊕al+1⊕⋯⊕ars(l,r)=al⊕al+1⊕⋯⊕ar.

    First let's reverse the operations. Instead of changing subarray [1,n][1,n] into [i,i][i,i], we try to expand [i,i][i,i] into [1,n][1,n]. For a subarray [l,r][l,r], we need to find an adjacent subarray (either [r+1,x][r+1,x] or [x,l−1][x,l−1]) which has a smaller xorxor value than s(l,r)s(l,r), and merge them.

    Let's look at the maximum most significant bit of all aiai. Let cntcnt be the number of aiai s that have this bit. Let b1,b2,…,bcntb1,b2,…,bcnt be all ii that aiai has this bit.

    If cntcnt is odd, then only b1,b3,…,bcntb1,b3,…,bcnt are valid.

    If cntcnt is even, then all i∈[b2k−1+1,b2k−1]i∈[b2k−1+1,b2k−1] are invalid. We can merge all ab2k−1  ,ab2k−1  +1,…,ab2kab2k−1  ,ab2k−1  +1,…,ab2k into one number, which is s(b2k−1,b2k)s(b2k−1,b2k). Then, the maximum most significant bit is gone and we go on to solve the problem for a smaller bit. What I still can't figure out is how to solve for abiabi. Maybe it could be solved using Segment Tree.

https://codeforces.com/contest/1863/submission/221150801

Can I ask? Why my 7th test got a negative number? I didn't see any "out range" in my variety (for example, when I'm finding the missing number, I'm using long long)

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Omars
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Omars
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +10

    (n+1) * n will overflow because n is an int. Cast n to long long or declare it that way from the beginning

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    7etuPr0mK_X-VPA.8-ER1SYJ
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    7etuPr0mK_X-VPA.8-ER1SYJ
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    When you multiply (n+1) and n, because they are both int values, it overflows before stored into variable missing. Just change one of them into long long then multiply.

    long long missing = (n + 1) * (n) / 2; should be changed to long long missing = 1ll * (n + 1) * (n) / 2; or long long missing = (long long)(n + 1) * (n) / 2;.

    → Reply

0

Can someone help me with P5? My idea was to perform a dfs to find pairs (s1,s2) of optimal start time and end time for each component of the directed graph. If d is the number of connected components in the graph, then we will have d such (s1,s2) pairs. Then, I sorted this vector of pairs and found the optimal time.

Issue is I am getting TLE on test case 18. I don't see which operation is being so expensive. Any help will be highly appreciated.

Source Code

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define ANAND ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0)
#define rep(i, m, n)  for (ll i = m; i < n; i++)

typedef long long ll;
typedef vector<ll> vec;
typedef vector<vec> vecc;
typedef vector<pll> vecp;

ll k;

void dfs(vecc& graph, ll root, vec& h, ll day, ll time, vec& visited, ll& s2) {

	if (visited[root] > day*k + time) return;

	visited[root] = day*k+time;

	s2 = max(visited[root], s2);

	for(auto& q: graph[root]) {
		if (h[q] < h[root]) {
			dfs(graph, q, h, day+1, h[q], visited, s2);
		} else {
			dfs(graph, q, h, day, h[q], visited, s2);
		}
	}
	
}


void SOLVE() {
	ll n, m;
	cin >> n >> m >>k;

	vec h(n+1); // hours of i is h[i]

	rep(i,1,n+1) cin >> h[i];

	vecc graph(n+1);
	rep(i,0,m) {
		ll a,b;
		cin >> a >> b;
		graph[a].push_back(b);
	}
	
	vec visited(n+1, -1); // visited stores the time required to be completed with inital value -1 (if unvisited)
	vecp sdata; // (s1,s2)

	ll newMax = -1;

	rep(i,1,n+1) {
		if (visited[i] != -1) continue;
		
		ll s1 = h[i];
		ll s2 = h[i];

		dfs(graph, i, h, 0, h[i], visited, s2);
		newMax = max(newMax, s2);
		sdata.push_back({s1,s2});
	}

	sort(sdata.begin(), sdata.end());

	ll d = sdata.size();
	ll ans = newMax - sdata[0].first;

	rep(i,0,d-1) {
		ll newMin = sdata[i+1].first;
		newMax = max(sdata[i].second + k, newMax);
		ans = min(ans, newMax - newMin);
	}
	cout << ans << "\n";
}


signed main() {
	ANAND;
	
	ll _;
	cin >> _;
	while(_--) {
		SOLVE();
	}
	return 0;
}

In problem E can anybody help me understand why every node's time is going to be updated at most once ??

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    OldBrilliantMate
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    OldBrilliantMate
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    If you start the process on the 2nd day instead of the first, every value will increase by 1. So, if you had started earlier (meaning on any time of the day 1), values couldn’t have been bigger. Hence, they can increase only once.

    → Reply

I just want to ask what happens if the number in the array provided is not in 0 to n as it is obvious that it wil come in 0 to n+1 with leaving the last element but how can a person do that question because in c it is already given it is in 0 to n So what wil be the difficulty of the above with the present C?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    electric_boogaloo
    3 years ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    electric_boogaloo
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +11

    If the original array could have duplicates or values outside of range [0, n] the solution would be still pretty much the same. You can manually perform one operation on the original array, now all values are going to be MEX of some arrays of length NN, so that guarantees that all values are now in range [0, n], moreover, after performing such an operation there can't be two duplicate values in the array. All that's left to do now is to decrease k by one and calculate the cyclic shift for k modulo n + 1.

    → Reply

I tried to implement my solution in E problem but it was giving TLE on testcase 5. please can someone show the mistakes in my code.
my submission: link

Here I have tried to explain my logic:
1. sorted the given array of time at which quest will start and then mapped the indexes accordingly.
2. made an array ind to store indegree of every node of the graph.
3. then calculated the time needed if we start from each node with 0 indegree(as it can be started independently), using calc function.
x = no. of nodes with 0 indegree.
idx: contains the index of node with 0 indegree.
val: contains the time needed to complete the quest if we start from here (to complete all the quests that are dependent on the particular node).
4. used some prefix, suffix logic to get the final ans. (to decide at which point to start)

Radewoosh
3 years ago, show #
»
Radewoosh
3 years ago, hide # |
 
+18

I didn't get most of the edi, but I think that I is much easier (and I upsolved it this way):

I've stopped reading after getting to the point that we will take whole classes of adjacent paths. So let's connect them and make a directed edge from class of shorter paths to a class of longer paths if they contradict with each other. There surely is a way to extend one of the shorter paths into one of the longer paths if this is the case. So we might as well only use edges from a class to a class of paths longer by one. Aaaaaand this graph turns out to be a tree, where each vertex has a weight (number of paths in this class) and we have to pick a subset such that no pair ancestor-descendant is picked, so easy dp.

→ Reply

I think there is a typo in the editorial for E.

Where it says

"If we denote wvwv then it can be expressed as

max{wu:u−>v,cu+k>cv}max{wu:u−>v,cu+k>cv}

"

It should be minmin rather than maxmax since the goal is to find the first index that causes cvcv to increase by kk.
